issues.title: Add type-specific Clipboard methods
issues.body:
@justinmc As discussed in #60145...

Based on iOS 14's new [UIPasteboard APIs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard), we need to add type-specific methods to Flutter's [Clipboard](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/Clipboard-class.html) utility:

- `Clipboard.getImages`
Used most commonly in chat apps. Should implement `UIPasteboard` and `hasImages`. ([Docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard/1829411-hasimages)) 
- `Clipboard.getNumbers`
Used most commonly for auto-verifications. Should implement `UIPasteboard` and `detectPatterns`. ([Docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard/detectionpattern)) 
- `Clipboard.getStrings`
Used most commonly in journaling/editing apps. Should implement `UIPasteboard` and `hasStrings`. ([Docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard/1829416-hasstrings))
- `Clipboard.getUrls`
Used most commonly for deep-linking and auto-population of URLs. Should implement `UIPasteboard` and `hasURLs`. ([Docs](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard/1829410-hasurls))
comments: Thanks for creating this!
@justinmc Is this still open? I know #60145 was resolved a while back...

**This needs to be revisited!**
Starting iOS 16 a system alert is produced whenever an app tries to access the clipboard data asking the user for their consent. Typically, developers will just get the clipboard data and then parse it to see if it's the type they are looking for. 
However now, the developers cannot afford to do that as it will produce a new alert each time even if the user does not have a relevant clipboard type to what we are looking for.

For example, in Swift, we would normally use the Pasteboard API, if the app is trying to get just images it cause use something like this:
` let pb: Data? = UIPasteboard.general.data(forPasteboardType: "public.image")
`
This will return null if the user has something that's not an image (like text), and will **not try** to fetch the clipboard content hence not evoking a system alert.
@SethuSenthil It's only been 2 years. Give it time. 😜
@justinmc If you're too busy, I'd love to help out with a PR fixing this. A few directions could help me get started. I see you already implemented the `hasStrings` method
Adjusting team assignment; while there are iOS-specific aspects here, the overall type-specific clipboard API design should be cross-platform.
Somewhat related: PR https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/110052 did image insertion via the keyboard on Android (not the clipboard).
@simplenotezy Sorry I missed your comment, but if you're still up for it I'd be happy to help you fix this!

I made and published a package [uipasteboard](https://pub.dev/packages/uipasteboard) on pub.dev.
The package focuses on providing [iOS UIPasteboard](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipasteboard) access in the Flutter context.

## Use case
The use case is when a user is typing in private information. An example can be a password field in an app. This would be for mobile apps but also for web and desktop apps as it  makes more sense there.
<!--

## Proposal
The obscureText parameter in the TextFormField obscures the text when set to true. However, it shows the last typed character for a short amount of time before hiding it or until another character is typed. I would like to have the option to obscure all text, even the character that I just typed. Usually on mobile devices you would want to show the last typed character as the keyboard is small so they would need to see what they are typing, however, there are occasions where I would like to hide all characters. Another example is now that flutter is on the web and that desktop support is in alpha, I think that having the option to hide all characters, especially on desktop, would be a great addition to the TextFormField. issues.title: Support oversized WidgetSpans in TextFields
issues.body:
Original internal report: https://yaqs.corp.google.com/eng/q/7281814822109315072

Since TextFields assign their height via multiplying the number of lines by the preferred line height (determined by the TextStyle and StrutStyle), oversized WidgetSpans often have trouble fully rendering, resulting in clipped content and hit testing.

WidgetSpans rendering support was added to TextFields recently, but no new API was added to support the drastically different line heights and shapes WidgetSpans can have in a TextField.

We should provide additional API to allow this to be better controlled.

Clipping also causes unexpected rendering and hit testing behavior that should be better defined and in some cases fixed.
comments: I think this is preventing us from making an embedded widget accessible. Specifically, we're rendering math as an image within a `WidgetSpan`. Currently, it blindly scales down the image. As an initial experiment, I was trying to use `IntrinsicWidth` around the `TextField` to try to get it to size to content, but that just threw an error due to the widget reporting a size of 0. That led me to this bug.
@marcianx Can you provide some sample code that showcases the a11y problem you are facing with this? That will make it easier to determine the best solution for that problem.

To make the expectation more concrete: it looks like so:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/370338/195141225-c6e14324-4a16-4201-9ddc-0497950a7121.png)
Instead, we want it to span just the red-to-green half and want the green-to-blue half off to the right. And we want be able to scroll the `TextField` programmatically to show the appropriate part of the gradient (well, math image in the actual use-case) to sure that the cursor we render over the math is always visible (just like native `TextField`s).

issues.title: [Web] Korean IME composing range assertion failure after window blur (Cmd+Tab) during composition
issues.body:
## Steps to reproduce

1. Run a Flutter Web app with a `TextField` on Chrome (macOS)
2. Switch to Korean IME and start typing (e.g., type "한")
3. **While the character is still composing** (cursor is on the character, not yet committed), press **Cmd+Tab** to switch to another app
4. Switch back to the Flutter Web app
5. Press **Backspace**

## Expected results

Backspace should delete the composed character normally.

## Actual results

Backspace does not work. The following assertion error is thrown:

```
═══ EXCEPTION CAUGHT BY SERVICES LIBRARY ═══
Failed assertion: line 1155 pos 7: 'googValue.googcomposing.end <= googValue.text.length'

composingBase: 1, composingExtent: 2, text: 한
```

The `composing` range (1–2) exceeds `text.length` (1), causing an assertion failure. After this, Backspace and other key inputs stop working.

## Root cause analysis

When the browser window loses focus (`window.blur`), a `compositionend` event fires. This removes the composing text from the DOM, but **Flutter's internal composing range is not updated accordingly**. This creates a mismatch in `TextEditingValue`:

- `text.length` = 1 (the composed character was committed/shortened)
- `composingBase` = 1, `composingExtent` = 2 (still referencing the old composing range)

When the user returns and presses a key, `TextEditingValue.fromJSON()` hits the assertion before any app-level code can intercept.

## Workarounds attempted (all failed)

| # | Approach | Result |
|---|---------|--------|
| 1 | `addPostFrameCallback` after `_restoreFocus()` to clear composing | Too late, assertion already fired |
| 2 | `AppLifecycleState.inactive` to clear composing | Web doesn't fire `inactive` (only `hidden`) |
| 3 | `window.blur` event listener via conditional import + composing cleanup | No effect |
| 4 | `activeElement.blur()` to force IME commit + TextField key regeneration | IME switches to English + still fails |
| 5 | Focus restore with 50ms delay + composing cleanup | No effect |

The core issue is that the assertion fires in the platform channel → `TextEditingValue.fromJSON()` path, **before any app-level Dart code can intervene**.

## Key constraint

- English input: no issue
- Composing completed (cursor outside character): no issue
- **Only occurs when composing is active** (cursor on the character being composed)

## Environment

- Flutter 3.10+ (stable)
- Chrome on macOS
- Korean IME (macOS default)
- Reproducible with both CanvasKit and HTML renderer

## Related issues

- #65357 (composing range on web — fixed, but this edge case remains)
- #131023 (assertion failure with composing state — similar symptom, different trigger)
- #147282 (Japanese IME + Tab key on web — similar pattern of IME composition + special key)
comments:
Hi @tobedoit , Thanks for the report and root-cause analysis. 

I was able to successfully reproduce this assertion failure using the following steps:
1. Switch Mac keyboard to Korean (2-Set).
2. Type `g` `k` `s` (forms "한") in the `TextField` without pressing Space/Enter (leave in composing state).
3. Press `Cmd+Tab` to switch windows, then `Cmd+Tab` back to Chrome.
4. Press `Backspace` and the assertion fires.

**Triage Findings:**
* **Flutter:** 3.41.2
* **OS:** macOS 26.3
* **Browser:** Chrome

Routing to `team-web` to investigate the `compositionend` event sync issue.

issues.title: TextSelection doesn't support TextAffinities for non-collapsed selection
issues.body:
[TextSelection](https://master-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextSelection-class.html) has a single [affinity](https://master-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextSelection/affinity.html) property, and according to its docs, it only applies when collapsed: > If the text range is collapsed and has more than one visual location (e.g., occurs at a line break), which of the two locations to use when painting the caret. I think that this may be an oversight and a source of bugs, especially when using a keyboard. For example, these selections are different, but Flutter has no way to distinguish between the two:
extent TextAffinity.downstream extent TextAffinity.upstream Flutter treats them both as: 
This text wra|ps because it's
|too long.

This text wra|ps because it's |
too long.
### Why does it matter? When using keyboard shortcuts that jump to the beginning/end of a line, it's important to know what line the base and extent are on. Consider doing cmd-shift-right (expand to end of line) in my example in the table above. If the extent is upstream, the selection shouldn't change. If it's downstream, it should select to the end fo the field. Always assuming one or the other affinity won't always make sense. ### Related https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/87973/files#r685632915 https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/85653
comments:
I think whether we need separate text affinities for each end point depends on the visual appearance of the selection/caret.

On desktop platforms a non-collapsed selection generally doesn't have a "handle" so it doesn't need text affinity to disambiguate the different visual locations of each endpoint with the same logical position. From what I'm seeing on macOS, non-collapsed selections do not have cursor affinity there, since a non-collapsed text selection on macOS is visually represented by a few highlighted rectangles. Having `TextAffinity.upstream` for soft line breaks in this case would just mean an empty rectangle being placed at the end of the previous line, which does make any difference visually.

And in some text editors the caret is visually the same as a single character selection. In that case there's no ambiguity when the caret is placed around a soft linebreak or a bidi boundary.

I don't have a tablet with me that has both selection handles and hardware keyboard support. I wonder if iPad supports the same selection shortcuts as macOS, if it does then we'll probably have to add text affinity for both end points.

I also don't have an iPad unfortunately, but we should check that at some point.

I agree with you about Mac not needing affinity for non-collapsed selections after playing around with it.  I even tried some tricky stuff like selecting from the start of one line to the previous line, then trying to cmd-shift-right to jump to the end of the line.  It doesn't do anything, because it seems that Mac is assuming that the base is upstream.

Maybe we can also make some explicit and documented assumptions about the affinity of the base/extent when not collapsed.

issues.title: Create CommandOrControl so ctrl+c in Windows == command+c in Mac
issues.body:
LogicalKeyboardKey's `controlLeft` on Windows and Linux means ctrl (like ctrl+c), but on macOS control is something else entirely different. For things like ctrl+c, the correct is command+c, which is Windows' Windows key (`metaLeft`). This gets instantly confusing.

I've seen apps with shortcuts broken because the developer only tested on Windows/Mac and didn't realize control works completely different across both.

My suggestions: somehow we could have a LogicalKeyboardKey "correct/expected" control and meta so developers and users wouldn't be confused or even worry about it. This affects even Flutter Web. No one is going to do `if (Platform.macOS) metaLeft else controlLeft` because it is going to take too long for them to realize this is necessary. It seems like a bug, even though the logical key is 100% correct (from logical point of view).
comments: CC @dkwingsmt
I agree, I also think the `if (Platform.macOS) metaLeft else controlLeft` pattern is too much boilerplate. Let me see if we can come up with a utility class.
Hey @dkwingsmt, that code wouldn't work because there is no dart:IO in Flutter Web, so it would be [even more complicated](https://stackoverflow.com/a/67644049).
cc @cbracken to consider for the desktop stable release

Yeah I've been considering this for a while. It's not a lot to implement, and for sure important. Just hard to name.

Let's discuss naming. We need a name for the activator class, one for the "control but meta for macOS" field, and one for the "meta but control for macOS" field. My latest thought is 
```
OsSingleActivator(
  this.osControl,
  this.osMeta,
...);
```
Or 
```
AdaptiveSingleActivator(
  this.primary,
  this.auxiliary,
...);
```

I prefer the first one. I like the idea of `controlCommand` or `macOSCommand` but they don't work well with meta (metaControl would be confusing 🙁) so I prefer your suggestions `osControl`/`osMeta`. Maybe instead of `os` it could be `nativeControl` but maybe native invokes the idea of something low level, which is not exactly this case.
/cc @justinmc for comments. Is there an existing bug for this? Alternatively, does an alternative solution for this already exist?

Currently we maintain separate maps for our default text editing shortcuts.  [Here is Mac's meta+c](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/ab51a0260d29d50800ae5c9665ecb01883a9b503/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L409) and [here is Windows control+c](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/ab51a0260d29d50800ae5c9665ecb01883a9b503/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/default_text_editing_shortcuts.dart#L460).  There is no slicker way right now that I'm aware of.  If this issue gets fixed maybe we can simplify those maps.
Idea 2: we could somehow unify 'delete' with 'backspace' as they are the same shortcut 98% of the time.
I face this issue just now and would really like an easier interface. How about "platformControl" and "platformMeta"?

I think this issue should be renamed to something like "Create a utility to create shortcuts that use cmd on Mac and ctrl on Windows/Linux". The title as written is not a bug; `ctrl+c` should not be handled as `cmd+c` on a Mac by default.

FWIW, Electron has a "CommandOrControl" activator for their shortcuts system which does exactly what's described here: https://www.electronjs.org/docs/latest/api/accelerator

A little wordy for my tastes but it gets the point across.

issues.title: Add keyboard input support for dates, times and durations to TextFormField/TextField
issues.body:
Hi,
For desktop and web it is essential that the TextFormField widget natively supports the format to input a **date**, a **timeofday**, a **date and time** and a **duration** by **keyboard**.
On desktop, this type of data is only occasionally entered through an external dialog box. 
Thanks.
Regards,
Jose
comments:
We have a Material date picker already, and these types of inputs are often very application specific.

It could be that a [TextInputFormatter](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextInputFormatter-class.html) might serve your needs as well.

Hi gspencergoog,

These types of inputs are not specific to certain applications, they are totally normal inputs in any desktop application. Any development environment, for example, Delphi, C # NET, ... has native support for these types of inputs. The native Windows GDI controls have these types of controls.
I don't know why you say they are specific to certain applications. 
They are completely normal and it is really very strange that a framework like Flutter does not have these types of inputs.
I thought that Flutter had an aspiration to be a reference development environment for desktop applications, but maybe I was wrong.
I am a developer of desktop applications with many years of experience and it is really very strange that these types of inputs do not exist. 
Regards.

I just mean that the visual presentation of those fields seems very application specific: some want it inline in the field, some want a separate dialog, some a dropdown, etc.

Are you asking for field validation/filtering of date/time input? Or a separate form field (not just an extension to the text field) for that kind of input?

Just because we aspire to it, doesn't mean we've achieved it yet. :-) Your request seems like a good idea.

In desktop applications it is very easy and fluid to input dates, times and durations using the keyboard, accounting programs, billing programs, and many other types of programs use direct entry of the date using the keyboard without having to open another dialog box, which does not mean that a picker is also usually incorporated simultaneously in the event that the operator wants to view a calendar or a clock, ...
Generally, in the introduction of the data, the simultaneous validation of the data that is input takes place.
There are usually 2 display modes, one when the field has the focus, in that mode the date is displayed in a **simple mode** prepared for the input of the characters, and another mode, when the field does not have the focus, in which your you can see the date, time or duration in **expanded mode** according to the format selected by the developer, in that mode the month can be seen in characters, etc.
I do not know if I have explained myself clearly. 
In the mobile world, the pickers are normal, but in the desktop world it is very normal to combine the keyboard input function with the pickers. I am convinced that the developers will thank you very much for this functionality. And thanks to these features it will be possible to create traditional desktop applications that operators are used to handling. 
I think a good extension to the TextFormField / TextField widgets might suffice. 
Thank you very much for all your interest. :-)
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

issues.title: Incorrect selection area in RTL TextField.
issues.body:
A dot at the end of a line that is followed by another line makes the text selection area incorrect!
First, create a simple app with an RTL multiline TextField. (or use my sample code.)

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Add a line with this text: "Select a word and see the selected area."
2. Add another line
3. Double click on the "word" to select it.

Expected:
The selection area should match the "word" area.

Actual:
The selected area is not matched with the "word" area.

<details>
<summary>Sample code</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() =>
  runApp(MaterialApp(
    home: Scaffold(
      body: Column(
        mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
        children: [
          TextField(
            controller: TextEditingController(text: '\nSelect a word and see the incorrect selected area.\n'),
            textDirection: TextDirection.rtl,
            maxLines: null,
          ),
          TextField(
            controller: TextEditingController(text: '\nSelect a word and see the correct selected area.\n'),
            maxLines: null,
          ),
        ],
      ),
    ),
  ));
```
</details>

issues.title: [web] - Password managers can't autofill until after focusing on inputs due to lazy-loaded inputs
issues.body:
On the web, password managers have the ability to autofill forms either automatically, or manually through context menus and their chrome extensions. 

Currently, autofill via password managers in Flutter only work through context menus.  This is because input elements are only created after being focused on in our current text editing system. This prevents password managers from filling out forms automatically or through their chrome extensions until after an input in the form is focused on.  

One potential solution being explored to resolve this is https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/120613.

issues.title: Integrate inputAccessoryView capabilities on iOS/iPadOS/macOS/tvOS
issues.body:
## Use case

On iOS/iPadOS/macOS/tvOS, it is possible to make use of the `inputAccessoryView` property of `UIResponder` (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiresponder/1621119-inputaccessoryview) to attach a custom view on top of the OS keyboard.

This is the way to correctly attach an element natively on top of the keyboard and produce a native look&feel.

## Proposal

Since the framework itself is providing the `TextField` and `TextFormField` widgets, which will trigger the keyboard to appear if focused, the implementation of the native component should include the property `inputAccessoryView`.

issues.title: [Android 17] Improve CJKV physical keyboard accessibility
issues.body:
### Background

Android 17 improves the accessibility of physical keyboards for Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese input. To take advantage of these improvements, Flutter needs to add more information to the accessibility events it sends to Android.

### Work

Flutter's [AccessibilityBridge.createTextChangedEvent](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/24e6fedcaf3b255d9cfe285e62ab921fb9ef46d9/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/view/AccessibilityBridge.java#L2091) needs to be updated to call [`AccessibilityEvent.setTextChangeTypes`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/accessibility/AccessibilityEvent#setTextChangeTypes(int)) with the following values: 


1. `AccessibilityEvent.TEXT_CHANGE_TYPE_IN_COMPOSITION`, if the changed text was in the composing region
2. `AccessibilityEvent.TEXT_CHANGE_TYPE_COMMITTED_BY_IME`, if the IME committed text, even if the text is unchanged
3. `AccessibilityEvent.TEXT_CHANGE_TYPE_CONVERSION_SUGGESTION_SELECTED_BY_IME`, if the text changed as a result of a candidate being selected in the candidate window for the composing region.


To determine the text change type of `TEXT_CHANGE_TYPE_CONVERSION_SUGGESTION_SELECTED_BY_IME`, Flutter can use the new [`TextAttribute.isTextSuggestionSelected()`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/TextAttribute#isTextSuggestionSelected()) API to determine if an IME's candidate is currently selected.

The following methods on Flutter's [`InputConnectionAdaptor`](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/24e6fedcaf3b255d9cfe285e62ab921fb9ef46d9/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/plugin/editing/InputConnectionAdaptor.java) will need to be updated to override the new methods that accept a `TextAttribute` argument:

1. [InputConnectionAdaptor.setComposingText](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/24e6fedcaf3b255d9cfe285e62ab921fb9ef46d9/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/plugin/editing/InputConnectionAdaptor.java#L202) needs to updated override [this](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/InputConnection#setComposingText(java.lang.CharSequence,%20int,%20android.view.inputmethod.TextAttribute))
2. [InputConnectionAdaptor.setComposingRegion](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/24e6fedcaf3b255d9cfe285e62ab921fb9ef46d9/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/plugin/editing/InputConnectionAdaptor.java#L196) needs to be updated to override [this](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/InputConnection#setComposingRegion(int,%20int,%20android.view.inputmethod.TextAttribute))
3. [InputConnectionAdaptor.commitText](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/24e6fedcaf3b255d9cfe285e62ab921fb9ef46d9/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/plugin/editing/InputConnectionAdaptor.java#L174) needs to be updated to override [this](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/InputConnection#commitText(java.lang.CharSequence,%20int,%20android.view.inputmethod.TextAttribute))

### Resources

* [go/a11y-ime-pk-1aaa-adoption](http://goto.google.com/a11y-ime-pk-1aaa-adoption), Android's internal doc (not public).

issues.title: Make sure the `TextEditingValue` in the platform text input plugin and the framework converge after concurrent edits
issues.body:
Currently platform text input plugins and the framework have their own local replicas of the document being edited by the user. A local update to one document will be sent to update the other (remote) document to keep both in sync, using a platform channel message. It is crucial that both replicas having the exact same state when all communications are finished, to make sure the platform (and the system services like IME, autofill, etc,.) and the framework are looking at the same document.

Our current strategy is to encode the entire document in each update message to the remote document and overwrite the remote document. To avoid sending the same document over and over (echoing), both the platform text input plugin and the framework have logic to track the last known state of the remote document and only send updates when the local document's state is different from the last known remote state. There's a (not so slim) chance the states of the 2 replicas diverge after concurrent edits on both documents: 

![document_sync drawio](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31859944/131902246-ef1905c1-33e2-45cd-ba90-cf8d917d326b.png)
*The text input plugin and the framework's documents are initially synced, both start from `S(0, 0)`. If a local edit brings the text input plugin's state to `S(1, 0)` and before the change can be synced to the remote document, the remote document also makes a change that brings its state to `S(0, 1)`, with the current implementation the 2 documents simply exchange states thinking the other document is in the same state.*

With the current implementation the consistency is likely to be quickly restored, when the next update arrives (thanks to the redundancy in each update message) , however this may still cause unexpected behaviors, for instance the IME's predictive text bar may show completely irrelevant suggestions when this happens.

As https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/87972 is being implemented it's likely that in the future we'd like to remove the redundancy in each diff-based update message to free up the bandwidth/memory, which will absolutely require both documents to converge (otherwise they're going to diverge further and further).
comments: /cc @Renzo-Olivares do you think this is going to be a road blocker for text editing value diffs you're working on, or it will still work reasonably well with the current implementation?

This shouldn't be too much of a blocker but definitely something we should consider especially if we want to send deltas from the framework to the engine. Right now we are only sending deltas the engine creates to the framework, and when the framework updates the engine it still overwrites the entire value with the new one. 

Right now we should keep the `oldText` property as part of the delta so a developer can ensure they are applying a delta to the correct editing state.

If a delta comes in from the engine and the `oldText` does not match the current editing state, then we should discard that batch of deltas/add logic to handle those deltas accordingly. We don't have to worry about this on the framework -> engine because we simply overwrite the value every time.

issues.title: [Proposal] First party support for input masks
issues.body:
## Use case

Recently I was looking into adding an input mask to a TextFormField.
Similar to how [Qt does it](https://doc.qt.io/qt-5/qml-qtquick-textinput.html#inputMask-prop).

Example:
An input with mask `00-00`, where `0` allows digits and the `-` separator is 
prefilled at the correct position (index 2 in the text of the controller).
So an empty value would be '-' and not ''.

While looking into implementation options I didn't find anything in the SDK documentation.
I did however come across the following options:

- implementing a `TextInputFormatter` that handles a mask (not sure if this works and/or if this is flexible enough)
- using a package like `extended_masked_text` that provides this through a custom `TextEditingController`
This package does not support having the separators already prefilled.

## Proposal

I'd propose the following:

Add a `TextEditingController` or `TextInputFormatter` that can accept an input mask.
Or introduce a code lab that shows how to do this? (providing it would not be _that_ difficult)

issues.title: Access to Shift-toggle Keys using LogicalKeyboardKey
issues.body:
## Use case

The [`LogicalKeyboardKey` docs](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/LogicalKeyboardKey-class.html) state the following:

>A class with static values that describe the keys that [...] represent logical keys, which are keys which are interpreted in the context of any modifiers, modes, or keyboard layouts which may be in effect.

This works for most keys, however, there is this whole class of **logical** keys that is not covered:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19204050/108902927-9cfa3980-7614-11eb-93c2-20d1f90c3251.png)

The symbols at the top of these buttons (`!`, `@`, `#`, `$`, `%`, etc.) are all **not covered** by `LogicalKeyboardKey`.  

If I use e.g. a [`LogicalKeySet`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/LogicalKeySet-class.html) as in:

```
LogicalKeySet(LogicalKeyboardKey.shift, LogicalKeyboardKey.digit8)
```

In order to achieve the `*` symbol (multiplication/asterisk), this might work for an English keyboard layout, however, it will not work for other keyboard layouts where the `*` symbol lives somewhere completely else.

## Proposal

In our use case (a math keyboard), we need to be able to respond to **logical** `*`, `/`, etc. keys.  
This seems to not be currently possible using `LogicalKeyboardKey`, although it logically should be.

In the end, it does not matter **how** this is implemented into the framework, but there should be away to respond to these logical keys.
comments: It is implemented, but not all keys have convenience statics defined.  For instance, the '&' key doesn't have a `LogicalKeyboardKey.ampersand` convenience defined, but you can still reference it as `LogicalKeyboardKey(0x00000000026, keyLabel: r'&')` (0x26 is the Unicode value for Ampersand) It's independent of keyboard layout: it'll return that key on a QWERTY keyboard as well as an AZERTY keyboard. On a QWERTY keyboard, `RawKeyboard.keysPressed` will also report that the shift key is down, however, so keep that in mind. And, since the `Shortcuts` widget wants to find out if the set of pressed keys matches the `LogicalKeySet` exactly, you'll have to define both entries in the key map (one with shift pressed, and one without) to work on all keyboards.

Thanks so much for your response @gspencergoog!

This means that the solution for this isssue would be:

* Documenting the SHIFT ambiguity.
* Adding the remaining convenience shortcuts.


Is that correct?

>it'll return that key on a QWERTY keyboard as well as an AZERTY keyboard.

Should this be closed in favor of https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/77048 ?

issues.title: Supporting platform accessibility actions for custom keyboards
issues.body:
## Use case
I'm trying to implement a custom keyboard and want to match accessibility actions of framework keyboards of Android/iOS but am unable to do so.

I'm using following `Semantics` for keys of custom keyboard.
```dart
Semantics.fromProperties(
  properties: SemanticsProperties(
    label: "some-semantics-label",
    keyboardKey: true,
)
```

### Expected Behaviour

**Android**
When Talkback is on, Android system keyboard doesn't use double tap to input the key instead when you tap on the key, its semantic label is pronounced by Talkback and the key is entered into the input field. On Flutter using `keyboardKey: true` on our custom widget does not reproduce similar behaviour and I'm unable to find a way to remove double tap behaviour on Android. 

Here's a video which demonstrates android's system keyboard accessbiility: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y2tT4jDRfWs

On Native Android, we achieve this using following piece of code
```kotlin
ViewCompat.setAccessibilityDelegate(view, object : AccessibilityDelegateCompat() {
    override fun onInitializeAccessibilityNodeInfo(host: View, info: AccessibilityNodeInfoCompat) {
        super.onInitializeAccessibilityNodeInfo(host, info)
        info.removeAction(AccessibilityNodeInfoCompat.AccessibilityActionCompat.ACTION_CLICK)
        info.isClickable = false
    }
})
```

**iOS**
On iOS, the default behaviour of system keyboard, when voice over is on, is to pronounce the key semantic label but activate it only on double tap. However this behaviour can be overriden in voice over settings by turning on a setting called `touch typing`, after which the key can be input using a single tap. Again using `keyboardKey: true` on our custom widget does not reproduce similar behaviour instead it always requires double tap to input key irrespective of voice over touch typing setting.

Here's a video which demonstrates various modes of iOS's system keyboard with accessibility: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1O2hwY-mGyQ

On Native iOS, we achieve this by using `keyboardKey` accessibility trait
https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiaccessibility/uiaccessibilitytraits/1620182-keyboardkey

**Note**
Full sample code: https://github.com/jitinsharma/flutter_custom_keyboard/blob/master/lib/main.dart#L115

## Proposal
The expectation is `keyboardKey: true` should follow accessibility actions of framework keyboards on each Platform, which would help in replicating these actions on any flutter widget.

This trait was originally added for fuchsia use case and are not used by any other platform

in Android
we need to set
https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/accessibility/AccessibilityNodeInfo#setTextEntryKey(boolean)

issues.title: Why is DropdownButtonFormField's onChanged required?
issues.body:
<!-- Thank you for using Flutter!

     If you are looking for support, please check out our documentation
     or consider asking a question on Stack Overflow:
      * https://flutter.dev/
      * https://api.flutter.dev/
      * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/flutter?sort=frequent

     If you have found a bug or if our documentation doesn't have an answer
     to what you're looking for, then fill our the template below. Please read
     our guide to filing a bug first: https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports
-->

## Steps to Reproduce

Try to initialize a `DropdownButtonFormField` without specifying the `onChanged` parameter.

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->

For `onChanged` to not be required and the widget to still work, which is consistent with other FormField widgets, such as `TextFormField`.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->

Currently the `onChanged` parameter is required and is seemingly used as a way to disable the widget if you set it to null. If you don't care about the onChanged event and just want to use the DropdownButtonFormField in a normal basic form, you still have to set onChanged to an empty function (e.g. `onChanged: (value) {}`) otherwise it won't update the FormState/stay enabled. This feels unintuitive to me since e.g. the `TextFormField` widget does not require you to specify `onChanged` unless you're interested in that event.

While I'm not that experienced with Flutter, it seems like it would make more sense to not use `onChanged` as a way of disabling a `DropdownButtonFormField` and instead let it purely be a way of letting the user do something when the field changes. I don't know if there are issues with that or if this odd current behavior is just a leftover from old logic? Seems easy enough to change it up (I could attempt to submit a PR for it).

**Proposed Changes:**
Add a `bool enabled` parameter to `DropdownButtonFormField` (consistent with TextFormField) and use that to decide whether to set the underlying `DropdownButton.onChanged` to control its enabled'ness.

In the future, it might make sense to add an `enabled` parameter to `DropdownButton` as well, as an explicit way of controlling whether it should be enabled or not.

issues.title: Material Component widget request: multi-select search field with auto-suggestion
issues.body:
## Use case
[RecipeRadar](https://www.reciperadar.com/) currently provides the user with the ability to enter multiple ingredients and/or kitchen equipment items that they would like to include or exclude when searching for recipes.

This functionality is built using a server-side auto-suggestion API that responds to the user's query text with suggested valid ingredient and equipment identifiers (and their corresponding human-readable names).

While the user-facing application does not use `flutter`, we are evaluating it as a possible next-generation application framework, particularly as [support for Progressive Web Apps](https://flutter.dev/web) as a target platform continues to develop.

One detractor that we have encountered is that `flutter` does not yet support a multi-selection auto-suggest search input.

We are currently using [select2's multi-select functionality](https://select2.org/getting-started/basic-usage#multi-select-boxes-pillbox) and have discovered existing native client-side functionality in [Android's MultiAutoCompleteTextView](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/widget/MultiAutoCompleteTextView).

## Proposal
A [Material Component widget](https://flutter.dev/docs/development/ui/widgets/material) that [implements](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/tree/0549ab239009e6f76f95881364e935cd345a4da7/packages/flutter/test/material) support for multi-valued text input, with support for auto-suggestion.

- [Android's MultiAutoCompleteTextView](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/widget/MultiAutoCompleteTextView) may provide a useful reference for design, accessibility and implementation concerns
- Support for population of values from a server-side API endpoint is required; we and likely other customers would be greatly aided during system migration if the component would support client-side transformation of the data returned by the endpoint
- User-selectde items will require both a user-accessible text description, and also a service-defined ID value (similar to the distinction between an HTML [option](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/option) field's innerHTML and its `value` attribute)
- Support for purely client-side autosuggestion and tokenization may be valuable for other customers (although it is not currently a requirement that we have)

## Relates to
- https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/17119
- https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/23827

issues.body:
While looking to fix up the last Cupertino test (test/cupertino/text_selection_test.dart) in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/181634  that had a cross import to Material, I ended up being blocked, because that test uses `SelectableText.rich` and there is no Cupertino equivalent of SelectableText yet.

We probably need a Cupertino version of this, to fix that test.

Part of https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/177415

cc @justinmc We can probably remove the Cupertino tests from the umbrella issue once https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/181634 lands, and replace it with this issue instead, since that is the only remaining point?
comments:
Hello, the text input team discussed this yesterday.

This is a very reasonable ask: today an app that uses Cupertino directly cannot easily create text that is selectable with the Cupertino text handles and context menu. Instead, the app is forced to import Material and use `SelectableText`.

However, @Renzo-Olivares pointed out some drawbacks of introducing `CupertinoSelectableText`:

1. `CupertinoSelectableText` won't be able to adapt to Android and show Android selection handles and context menus on that platform. Even if you're using Cupertino directly, you would likely want to use `SelectableText` as that does adapt to the target platform.
2. Long-term, we'd like to move away from `SelectableText`. We want to make it easy to use `SelectableRegion` directly, while having it magically adapt to whatever platform you might be targeting (iOS, Android, or even third-party ones like LG!).

1. Could we maybe move the SelectableText tests that are currently in Cupertino, under `test/material/cupertino` ? That subfolder is specifically included in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/181634 for the very purpose for cross library testing, in function of Material. So we'd have the Cupertino specific SelectableText tests there, until SelectableRegion is up to the task. (I can update that PR to do that)
2. Yes! Let's do that in the long term.

The `fyi-text-input` label is redundant with the `team-text-input` label.
The `triaged-design` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-design` label or `fyi-design` label.

That subfolder is specifically included in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/181634 for the very purpose for cross library testing, in function of Material. So we'd have the Cupertino specific SelectableText tests there, until SelectableRegion is up to the task. (I can update that PR to do that)

This sounds very reasonable to me, but I'll defer to @justinmc

If the hint text of a textfield is larger than the space available, it cuts off the text with ellipses. In normal use-cases this is reasonable enough, but there are some where this isn't - particularly when dealing with accessibility & large text sizes.

issues.title: Add a legacy-compatible input action that mimics pre-3.13 `TextInputAction.next` behavior
issues.body:
### Use case

This proposal suggests adding a new `TextInputAction` value or an opt-in mechanism to replicate the legacy behavior of `TextInputAction.next` from Flutter versions prior to 3.13.

In older versions, pressing Enter or "Next" on the keyboard would:
1. Move focus to the next focusable widget.
2. Immediately trigger `onPressed` on a button if it was the next widget.

After Flutter 3.13, the behavior has changed:
- Pressing Enter only moves the focus.
- A second press is needed to activate the button's `onPressed`.

This change breaks intuitive form workflows and degrades UX on desktop/web where Enter is commonly used to submit or confirm forms.

In Flutter ≥ 3.13, due to changes in `FocusTraversalPolicy`, this no longer works. The same setup requires two Enter presses to move and then activate the next control, even if it's a button.

**Alternatives I've considered**  

- **Create a separate function and call it in both `onFieldSubmitted` and the button's `onPressed`.**  
  While this works, it introduces additional boilerplate and duplicates logic across UI elements. For forms with multiple fields and buttons, this quickly becomes verbose and harder to maintain. It also goes against the declarative and implicit navigation model Flutter previously supported with `TextInputAction.next`.

- **Override `onEditingComplete` and manually call `requestFocus()` or manage focus manually.**  
  This approach is not intuitive, requires tracking focus nodes explicitly, and does not scale well across deeply nested widgets or dynamic forms.

- **Wrap `TextField` in a custom widget that handles the traversal manually.**  
  Custom wrappers can introduce inconsistencies and increase code complexity, especially when used in multiple places across large codebases.

- **Use `FocusTraversalGroup.of(context).next(...)` manually.**  
  The underlying `FocusTraversalPolicy` behavior changed post-3.13, and manual traversal may no longer guarantee the same seamless interaction across platforms and widgets.

### Proposal

**Option 1: Add a new TextInputAction enum value**
```dart
enum TextInputAction {
  ...
  legacyNext, // New value
}
```

**Option 2: Add a flag to opt into legacy behavior**
```dart
TextField(
  textInputAction: TextInputAction.next,
  legacyNextBehavior: true,
)
```
If the above cannot be implemented within the framework itself, kindly guide whether this behavior can be replicated reliably using public APIs — ideally without excessive boilerplate or focus node management.

issues.title: DropdownMenu does not clear or update selection when user types text
issues.body:
### Use case

Currently, the `DropdownMenu` widget allows the user to:

1. Select an option from the dropdown list (triggering `onSelected`).
2. Type arbitrary text into the input field to perform searching or filtering.
3. Tap out of focus and leave the arbitrary text visible without selecting anything or triggering any callbacks.

This is confusing as the text field is the primary feedback for which item has been selected yet it no longer reflects that. See [example](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/DropdownMenu-class.html#material.DropdownMenu.1).

### Proposal

I believe the most comprehensive solution would be:

```dart
DropdownMenu(
    autoSelect: true,       // Automatically select highlighted menu item if focus lost
    incompleteTextBehavior: // If no highlighted menu item, or autoSelect: false
        DropdownIncompleteTextBehavior.ignore, // Leave text as-is,  don't emit (current behavior)
     or DropdownIncompleteTextBehavior.emit,   // Leave text as-is,  emit `onSelected(null)`
     or DropdownIncompleteTextBehavior.clear,  // Clear text,        emit `onSelected(null)`
     or DropdownIncompleteTextBehavior.reset,  // Reset to previous, emit `onSelected(previous)`
)
```

`DropdownIncompleteTextBehavior.ignore` could then be deprecated with a view to removing in future, forcing the user to do something more sensible.
comments:
Adding this also to show what is happening with `onSelected`:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/38fc24fc-163f-4867-b5c6-e7bfc26df48d

---

Somewhat related there is no easy way to set the value of the `DropdownMenu<T>` to a `T` without manually getting the desired label and applying it to the `TextEditingController`.

In its current form the widget feels like a `TextField`/`DropdownButton` frankenstein, excelling at neither function. There is a dedicated [`Autocomplete`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Autocomplete-class.html) widget for when the text is the primary focus so I feel the `DropdownMenu` should exclusively be for making selections from the predefined entries.

I think having a customizability is a good move, although I don't think we should ever deprecate `ignore`. It's like the search bar of Github or Google where you can totally type words without submitting and, while it might seems "confusing", it's an easy behavior to understand.

issues.title: Add 'validator' handler at Form level to support cross field validation logic
issues.body:
### Use case

Most non trivial form data entry requirements will include the validation of data across multiple input fields.   Currently, there is no simple and effective way of adding form level validation using the Flutter 'Form' and 'xxxxxFormField' widgets. 

### Proposal

Add a 'validator' handler at the 'Form' widget level.

The validator method would be passed an array of global key objects,  representing the global keys set up for each data input form field.  Like... 

`Function String? (List<GlobalKey> keys);`
comments:
Thanks for proposing this @davoutuk.
This seems similar to what the `key` property in `Form` widget does and `validator` in `FormFields`. Can you help with more context as to how this will be different from these existing properties?
For cross-field validation, you could also check out adding a `TextEditingController` to each `FormField`, that way you can check the value of each field.

To summarize...

There currently is no simple way to implement cross field validation in Flutter forms.   
I would expect the call 

`    if (_formKey.currentState!.validate()) {
`
..... to cover both field level and form level validation logic.

The only solution at the moment is to delegate cross field validation to a specific field, and this requires that 'key' values be set into each required field.   Even then the logic may display in the wrong place - cross field validation errors would ideally be displayed in a separate area of the form,  not under a specific field

>    if (_formKey.currentState!.validate()) {
..... to cover both field level and form level validation logic.

Using ` if (_formKey.currentState!.validate()) {` with Formfield's `validator` does validate each Formfield and display validation errors under the specific field. Do you have a reproducible sample of where this is not the case? 
Kindly help with sharing this with us or let know if I seem to be misunderstanding your proposal

Take the following situation...
1).,  A form has three input fields 'A', 'B', and 'C'
2). There is business logic that needs to check the inputs made into all three fields.   For example, if A is greater than 3 then B must be no larger than C, and if C is greater than 5 then A must not be greater than 2.
3). Given this cross field business validation logic, it makes no sense to show the error against a particular field.  Instead the validation error message should be shown as a form level input validation error.
The `triaged-design` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-design` label or `fyi-design` label.

issues.title: TextField.obscureText does not correctly handle complex characters like 👨‍👩‍👦
comments:
It looks like the current behavior is intentional. See:

* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/122381#issuecomment-1482042620
* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/123366

EDIT: I discussed this with Justin offline. We agreed to update Flutter to only produce a single obscured character for complex characters like 👨‍👩‍👦 .

IIRC it was intentional that obscure text is considered separated code points even if they form an EGC. The fewer interactions between characters there are the less information the password field gives away about the existing entry when you insert / delete.

But in general it should be discouraged to use EGCs or combining characters (or even characters not in the BMP) in your password. I tried on macOS and iOS and couldn't enter emojis at all.

We discussed this more offline. Here were some interesting findings by @LongCatIsLooong:

1. Chromium switched from showing an obscured character per code point to an obscured character per extended grapheme cluster. However, it's not clear how they evaluated this change: https://issues.chromium.org/issues/40417720#comment37, https://chromium-review.git.corp.google.com/c/chromium/src/+/547541 
2. https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8265 says that passwords should be NFC normalized. So if you use a combining character for `ï` in a password (aka decomposed), it'll be normalized to its single code point equivalent (cmoposed).
3. `UITextField` does show 8 dots for 👨‍👩‍👦, but inserting / deleting will clears the text field first

issues.title: Add integration tests for IME input
issues.body:
Our text input integration testing doesn't cover IME-based input such as for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. At the moment, this is covered by non-platform-specific unit tests.

We should include tests that:
1. Set the input method to an IME-based input method.
2. Send a sequence of key events such that are handled by the IME.
3. Verify the text editing contents, in particular, the composing region.

A few things to test:
* Ensure arrow cursor movement is restricted to the composing region when composing (in Chinese/Japanese)
* Test composing in Flutter, then switching to a non-Flutter textfield, composing there, and switching back to flutter. See https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/31879.
* Ensure backspace handling is correct while composing.
* Ensure Escape key handling is correct.
* Verify keyboard navigation of candidates menu is possible (and not captured by Flutter).

issues.title: Support for rendering custom carets in text fields
issues.body:
### Use case

I want to build a UI that's similar to the video in [this Tweet](https://x.com/avstorm/status/1696401608458764517).

I've also attached the video here in case the Tweet goes missing

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/443d4da0-edf6-4b35-a33d-82468e1d101e

Essentially that demo uses the caret (cursor) in the text field as a progress bar to indicate whether the password strength is good enough, or the max number of chars in a text field are reached (think Twitters 280 char limit).

### Proposal

The `RenderEditable` class already takes two parameter to draw either above or below it as seen here:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/4ff2deafd4b9d9b8bf030db8b2163b49ecdccf10/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/editable.dart#L335-L336

The caret itself is also a `RenderEditablePainter` as seen here:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/4ff2deafd4b9d9b8bf030db8b2163b49ecdccf10/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/editable.dart#L2954-L3112

However, those parameters can't be set from `TextField` et al.

I see two options how to proceed here:

1. Wire up `RenderEditable.painter` and `RenderEditable.foregroundPainter` so that it can be passed via `TextField`, `CupertinoTextField` and friends.
2. Make `_CaretPainter` a public API and wire it up to `TextField`, `CupertinoTextField` and friends.

-----

Related issue https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/120836
comments:
That looks pretty cool and I hope it's something we can enable people to build in Flutter.

Allowing .painter and .foregroundPainter to be passed in might open up a can of worms... I lean option 2 but I'd need to take a closer look. Anyway this is an interesting proposal.
I'm up for contributing this when there's a decision how to proceed

This could be done somewhat similar to the [`InteractiveInkFeatureFactory`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/InteractiveInkFeatureFactory-class.html). 
So e.g. we make `_CaretPainter` a public class (and give it a well-defined interface) that can be configured via [`InputDecorationTheme`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/InputDecorationTheme-class.html) or [`TextSelectionThemeData`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextSelectionThemeData-class.html) from which it eventually is passed to the `RenderEditableText`.
I think that approach makes sense. I'll see if I can gather other thoughts and I'll post back next week.

issues.title: Refactor FormField into separate abstract and concrete classes
issues.body:
Right now `TextFormField` extends `FormField` and that's the only place in Flutter where a class extends a concrete subclass of `StatefulWidget`. We should take the parts of `FormField` that `TextFormField` uses and refactor them into an abstract subclass of `StatefulWidget` that they both extend.

/cc @xster @abarth
comments: If we do this we have to be careful to do it in a backwards-compatible way to avoid breaking API stability.
What's wrong with extending a concrete subclass of StatefulWidget?

If our tagline is composition > inheritance I think we should discourage the pattern of making helpers that extend concrete subclasses of `StatefulWidget`, because it encourages devs to treat composition as expensive and rely on inheritance.

1. I would normally expect to be able to pass a `GlobalKey<TextFormFieldState>` as the `key` argument to the `TextFormField` constructor but `TextFormFieldState` isn't a type.

1. I would normally expect that `new TextFormField(autofocus: true).autofocus == true` but I get "the getter 'autofocus' is not defined."

1. If we add new named constructor arguments to `FormField`, and there are subclasses out there that had constructor arguments of the same name but with different types, the consumer will now get "invalid override" errors making this a breaking change.

1. Things get more dangerous if the subclass overrides `build()`. If we add new named constructor arguments to `FormField`, subclasses that had constructor arguments of the same name and types might result in the subclass's version being unintentionally used in two places; once in the subclass and once by FormField in the call to `super.build()`.

1. Overriding `createState` would extend the brittleness as well.

@sethladd
I agree that we should fix those issues, and I'm happy to do so by not using inheritance, but I'm not convinced that those issues are implied by the use of inheritance from a concrete class.
I'm fine with this not being a hard-and-fast rule as long as there are Reasons.
The base class taking a `builder` is also really awkward because you need to be able to create the closure for `super(builder:...)` without access to `this`.
If this would require an API change, it's not happening. But I'm leaving this open so that we can study it in more detail when we have more time.

issues.title: SystemContextMenu support outside of editable text?
issues.body:
### Use case

Currently, SystemContextMenu is only supported in the context of a text input field. It doesn't even work when readOnly is true, due to the lack of an active TextInputConnection (see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/171242). So when users select text in a read only field, or in a SelectionArea, they currently see the Flutter-rendered menu, which is not quite identical to the SystemContextMenu.

### Proposal

Flutter should support showing the SystemContextMenu for non-editable text and even other cases, like long-pressing an image to show a context menu.

Under the hood, the context menu is inserted into the UITextInputView (explained in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/171242#issuecomment-3046645634), so it would be necessary to refactor this to support insertion into something like the FlutterView.
comments: A little confused here... afaik SystemContextMenu in editable text was added to solve the secure paste problem. Doesn't arbitrarily showing the system context menu go against Flutter's core principle of maximally 'owning' the UI?

> A little confused here... afaik SystemContextMenu in editable text was added to solve the secure paste problem. 

Non-editable fields are not paste-able, so I guess it is up to the framework team's decision then. I'm happy either way. 

> Flutter's core principle of maximally 'owning' the UI?

This reminds me of [a discussion on the same topic](https://docs.google.com/document/d/16-8kn58h_oD902e7vPSh6W20aHRBJKyNOdSe5rbAe_g/edit?resourcekey=0-gVdJ3fbOybV70ZKeHU7fkQ&tab=t.0#heading=h.iamvrpjs1n1). I had a `TODO: add source` there but I wasn't able to find any source. Wondering if you have any?

That's a good point to address in this issue. I do think we should prefer Flutter-drawn UI where possible. I don't have a source for that but I'll write something up.

In this case, the fact that the look and feel of the context menu is different depending on the value of readOnly makes me worry about the user experience and feel inclined to allow the SystemContextMenu in that case. Maybe this isn't commonly noticed by users in reality though and it's not worth the change.
A thought from triage: In the long term, it's probably not feasible to completely get rid of the Flutter-rendered context menu, for platforms that don't have a built-in context menu.

issues.title: TextField: allow overriding contextMenuBuilder globally via ThemeData
issues.body:
### Use case

In certain Android app configurations, it is desirable to suppress the long-press context menu on text fields entirely — for example, in focused or restricted UI environments where exposing system-level UI (share sheet, app chooser dialogs) is undesirable or disruptive to the user experience.

The only option today is to pass `contextMenuBuilder` explicitly to every `TextField` and `TextFormField` in the codebase, which is impractical at scale. More critically, this approach does not cover Flutter's own widgets that embed `TextField` or `TextFormField` internally, such as `TimePickerDialog`, `DatePickerDialog`, `SearchAnchor`, `DropdownMenu`, or any third-party package using `TextField` internally. There is no way to suppress the context menu in those without forking Flutter or the package.

A wrapper widget approach (e.g. a custom `AppTextField`) was considered, but it only solves the problem for widgets you own — not for Flutter-internal or third-party widgets. There is no package on pub.dev that solves this at the theme level.

### Proposal

Add a `contextMenuBuilder` field to `TextSelectionThemeData`, which `TextField` and `TextFormField` would resolve before falling back to `_defaultContextMenuBuilder`:

```dart
// In TextSelectionThemeData:
final EditableTextContextMenuBuilder? contextMenuBuilder;

// In TextField's build:
contextMenuBuilder ?? Theme.of(context).textSelectionTheme.contextMenuBuilder ?? _defaultContextMenuBuilder
```

This would allow a single global override at the `MaterialApp` level:

```dart
MaterialApp(
  theme: ThemeData(
    textSelectionTheme: TextSelectionThemeData(
      contextMenuBuilder: (context, editableTextState) => const SizedBox.shrink(),
    ),
  ),
)
```

This must be provided by Flutter directly, as it requires changes to `TextSelectionThemeData`, `TextField`, and `TextFormField` internals. It cannot be replicated by a pub.dev package.

@zoechi, @HansMuller, @goderbauer this issue is definitively not a duplicate of #12675.

This issue only want support of TextSpan in TextField widgets, for example, I have a custom emoji font, and I use it in every Text widgets of my chat application using TextSpan. The only glitch there is in TextField. With no support of TextSpan, platform specific emoji appear and some times not because the platform doesn't support the emoji (our chat run on mobile, web and desktop using go-flutter) and the final result is not **fully unified**. 

I don't want a full featured RichTextEditor with bold, italic and other cool stuff, I don't want a super toolbox allowing to customize the content of the editor. I just want a simple TextField with support of TextSpan.

Thanks by advance for considering this request 🙂

Then using a listened, you can clone the text as you are writing and the effect is just the same as if you would be writing in the ``TextFormField``.

Some things like ``maxLines`` might break this approach, but for my multiline scenario it certainly did the trick. 

Support for this would be really nice from the platform.

issues.title: Keys don't map correctly when using "Dvorak - QWERTY ⌘" layout on macOS
issues.body:
![Screenshot 2022-12-03 at 1 11 46 AM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/156888/205433468-f2e26625-38ed-46bb-b76f-560993c0714f.png)

There's a keyboard layout on macOS called "Dvorak - QWERTY ⌘". It's a Dvorak keyboard layout and the "v" key is where the "." key is for QWERTY. So in a normal Dvorak keyboard, ctrl-v, cmd-v etc all work by doing a combo with the "." physical key. But that layout is a bit annoying because one handed operations like ctrl-c, ctrl-v etc become two handed operations since c and v are on the right side of the keyboard. 

So "Dvorak - QWERTY ⌘" solves that by being a Dvorak layout most of the time, except when the command ⌘ modifier is held. Then for that duration, all keys remap to the QWERTY layout. So cmd-c is still on the "c" physical key (which normally maps to "j" on Dvorak). 

That works in all AppKit apps but doesn't work in Flutter. The macOS menu bar is actually highlighting Edit->Paste each time I press cmd-v, but there's no pasting in the Flutter app itself. Pressing the physical keys cmd-. (which is the "v" key in Dvorak layout) works.
comments:
That is an unexpected behavior, because I intentionally created this keymap planning algorithm so that latin layouts such as French and Dvorak will use the character values of the events as the shorcut inputs.

Do you mean that there is a Dvorak layout and a Dvorak-command layout which behave differently in terms of shortcuts?

Correct, there's 2 layers of "specialness" here. 
1- Respecting the soft mapping of the keys during normal typing. The physical "c" key should be a "j" key in Dvorak
2- Apple has a special layout that doesn't respect the soft mapping when the command modifier is held down. In that case, the physical "c" key should still be a "c" key in the "Dvorak - QWERTY ⌘" layout when the command modifier is held down.

@dkwingsmt Is that something that can be fixed at the level of your mapping or does the Shortcuts widget need to do anything?  I guess it's currently receiving cmd-j instead of cmd-c in the example @xster gave.

issues.title: Provide text deltas to TextEditingController
issues.body:
Text deltas became available recently, but to my understanding, it is available only if you create a text editor with `DeltaTextInputClient`. I think it is too much for most developers to implement their own editor.

## Use case

I've created a package with a `TextEditingController` that styles text according to some rules as you type. It works, but the longer the text is, the slower text changes become visible because it takes more time to parse the text.

If I have to implement a text editor to achieve the above, the API of my package must be changed. In addition, thinking about the fact that even the Flutter's standard TextField created by awesome people from the Flutter team still has bugs and is far behind native text input components in terms of quality, I doubt that general developers including me can create good editors on our own.

## Proposal

Is it possible to make deltas accessible from `TextEditingController`? It will become possible to debounce parsing by instead temporarily updating the text spans corresponding to only the changed parts.

issues.title: [Keyboard] Analyze keyboard layout and enforce mandatory logical keys
issues.body:
We should change the algorithm of deriving logical keys on Web, macOS, and Linus, so that they're more intuitive to use.

This aims to resolve the following problems:
- Shift-Digit keys are broken. For example, physical `Shift-Digit1` produces logical `ShiftLeft + exclamation`. Related:
  - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/90167
  - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/96022
- Digit keys are broken on French layout. For example, physical `Digit1` produces logical `ampersand`.
- Letter keys are broken on Russian layout. For example, physical `keyA` produces logical `ф`. Related:
  - https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/59065

Although it is still technically possible to define shortcuts for such scenarios, doing so requires platform- and language- dependent code, which is both unintuitive and cumbersome.

The goal can be demonstrated by the following table:

| When pressing physical keys...| on layout | Previous logical keys | New logical keys |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | US | `digit1` | `digit1` (unchanged) |
| Shift-1 | US | `shiftLeft + exclamation` | `shiftLeft + digit1` |
| 1 | French | `ampersand` | `digit1` | 
| Shift-1 | French | `shiftLeft + digit1` | `shiftLeft + digit1` (unchanged) |
| a | Russian | `ф` | `keyA` |


This is specially critical to non-latin layout users (such as Cyrillic letters) because their keyboard currently can not use any shortcuts, including Ctrl-C, Ctrl-V, Ctrl-A, etc.

You might ask: Then how do I know that `Shift-1` is an exclamation mark? Fear not. You can still get it from `{Raw,}KeyEvent.character`. However, this means you can not define shortcuts for `Shift-exclamation` or simply `exclamation` for US layout anymore. This might be resolved by adding another field to `{Raw,}KeyEvent`, but I'm still unsure whether to do so.

## Progress

- [x] Windows: doesn't need it.
- [x] macOS
- [x] Linux/GTK
- [x] Web
- [ ] iOS
- [x] Android: doesn't need it

issues.title: [web] Autofill forms aren't updated properly
issues.body:
In the web engine, when we receive a `TextInput.updateConfig` message from the framework, we don't properly update the autofill form and its fields.

Details:
- When the new config is [received](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L2145-L2147), we create a [new `EngineAutofillForm`](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L978-L981).
- That new `EngineAutofillForm` [creates its own `<form>`](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L206) element and all the fields.
- The new `<form>` element isn't appended to the DOM because we only append during initialization (e.g. [here](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L1591) and [here](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L1749)).
- The previous `<form>` element (that was appended during initialization before the updated config arrived) remains in the DOM with no updates applied to it.

cc @htoor3 @LongCatIsLooong
comments:
Great catch! A new form also gets created + appended [here](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L1079), but `placeElement()` gets overridden for Android, iOS, Firefox strategies.  So we probably have to make an explicit call to `placeForm()`.  

Maybe the best place to do that is [here](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/351470ce31cb4852b826d3ed361369191768f1f8/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text_editing/text_editing.dart#L1271) since this gets called whenever we receive `updateConfig` messages.

issues.title: Integrate with Smart Text Selection APIs
issues.body:
Android has an open source [libtextclassifier API](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/external/libtextclassifier/+/master) which is used for smart text selection:

![image2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6687929/46423828-59719e80-c6ec-11e8-8c1d-733635afcaad.gif)

The text selection API is C++ and can potentially be used with iOS but there are a couple of problems:
- The library is part of AOSP so I am not sure if it can be included in Flutter as a first class dependency.
- They generate Intents on Android to open up relevant actions (such as Map). So the current recommended way of integration on Android is via the [Java API](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/frameworks/base/+/refs/heads/master/core/java/android/view/textclassifier/TextClassifier.java).
comments:
More details (I chatted with the team that owns this):

> Since the links are all Android APIs, I assume this currently does not work for iOS and is only available as a Java library.

The core library is in C++ and open source (as linked above) but that doesn't include things like intent generation. That is: when there's an address selected, we currently generate the intents for opening maps etc., which you'll want to use. So practically speaking yes, this is a Framework Java API.

> What kind of overhead does this introduce to text selection? Do the models execute on device?

It's all on device and inference varies depending on how much text there is to process, but it's usually between 5-15ms on Pixel 2 and other O devices.

> Is there a central mechanism so the user can opt out which we would have to integrate with?

No, the feature is always enabled.

The team also clarified that it is unlikely that this feature will make it to iOS by end of 2019 so we would expose this as an Android only feature (or do something clever to bring it to iOS before they themselves do it ;)).
I think this issue is missing the label `a:fidelity`

issues.title: `isVisible` should be public on `SystemContextMenuController`
issues.body:
### Use case

`SystemContextMenuController.show` will hide and show the context menu if `targetRect` differs by even a fraction of a pixel.

We are building a text field that can be visually stretched by the user (similar to Liquid Glass), and would like control over this behavior, as not every transformation should lead to the system context menu disappearing and appearing again.

We would like to work around this by calling `show()` only if the menu is not currently being displayed.
However, we don't have any safe way to do that, as we cannot know from the outside whether the menu is currently being shown, since `isVisible` is marked `@visibleForTesting`.

### Proposal

Make `isVisible` a public getter, so it's safe to consume and would constitute a breakage if removed at a later point.
comments:
@timcreatedit we'll also likely need to be able to query the current global rect for the currently visible toolbar, because one part of the app won't be able to prevent other parts of the app from also showing the global toolbar. In other words, UseCaseA needs to be able to verify that the visible toolbar is positioned for UseCaseA, and not UseCaseB.

Furthermore, a concept of ownership would probably help, too.

Hello, thanks for creating this issue! @Renzo-Olivares's thoughts from triage: a potential gotcha here is that currently the system context menu can be dismissed by the platform itself. As a result, the framework's view of whether the system context menu is visible or not can be out of sync. A solution might be to use something like passthrough views ([example](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uipopoverpresentationcontroller/passthroughviews?language=objc)) - this would allow us to prevent the platform from dismissing the system context menu. We'd be able to control whether the system context menu is visible or not entirely from the framework. That is likely a prerequisite for this issue.

issues.title: How do we simulate text editing deltas?
issues.body:
I'm working on an IME simulator so that developers can test against delta-based text editing in widget tests.

I think we may be missing an important tool to make this happen. Please let me know if I'm missing something.

To send a delta, we must send the current text, called `oldText`. We also need to know the current selection to determine what some of the delta values should be. How do we obtain this information so that we can assemble a delta?

**Options:**

1. Implement an entire IME in Dart. This is a huge undertaking and I don't think it should be necessary.
2. Wrap the existing channel handler with a custom handler and intercept the messages about selection and text. I don't see any way to do this because I don't see how to get a reference to the current channel handler, which means I can't wrap the current channel handler with my own decorator.
3. Use a `Finder` to locate a widget that implements `DeltaTextInputClient` and query the information. This approach works. However, in `SuperTextField` we don't implement `DeltaTextInputClient` on a `State` object. Instead, we implement that API in our `ImeAttributedTextEditingController`. As a result, no `Finder` will ever find that controller.
4. Globally access the current text input connection, get a reference to the current client, and query the `currentTextEditingValue`. This isn't possible because there is no public access to the global input connection. It exists statically, but those references are private.

So in our test tooling, how can we assemble appropriate deltas when sending them through the system channel, as if we were the platform?

issues.title: [Text Selection] `TextSelectionDelegate` should not expose direct access to `TextEditingController`'s `TextEditingValue`
issues.body:
Instead, it should operate on the text/selection from the previous frame as it is what the user sees.

Things that may prevent this from happening:
- Currently in the `TextSelectionControls` implementation we sometimes do a side-effect and try to observe it immediately: For instance, in response to certain gestures we first select a word and the text selection menu should show up with the "cut" and "copy" options.
comments:
The `TextSelectionDelegate` class is also used by `RenderEditable`. `RenderEditable` does not know the `TextEditingValue` used to build its `TextSpan`, or how to convert its `TextSpan` back to `TextEditingValue` (`_plainText` works for the default `TextEditingController.buildTextSpan` but if you want to do something less conventional, e.g., https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/80185, `_plainText` does not work). So either 
- `RenderEditable` needs to know what its `TextEditingValue` is, and how to build the text span, and converting `TextPosition`s from/to engine text offsets
- or non-idempotent APIs (delete, change the current selection, etc.) need to be removed from `RenderEditable`, or be changed to idempotent APIs (e.g.: `selectWord` => `getWordRangeAtPosition`), and let whoever knows how to do the `TextSpan` <=> `TextEditingValue` conversion handle the update.
I remember the plan was to move the non-idempotent APIs up to the editable text, so I think we should go with the second proposal?

I agree, RenderEditable does not need to know the raw TextEditingValue, especially since this value is changed by both the IME as well as various framework entities. It makes more sense to consolidate it in editable text where other similar operations are made.
