issues.title: Improve documentation of `TextField`'s `decoration` property interplay with `Theme.inputDecorationTheme`
issues.body:
### Use case

The [current documentation of flutter's `TextField`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField-class.html) contains no mention of the fact that any explicit `decoration` is merged with whatever additional decorations are defined in the surrounding theme, making it unnecessarily difficult to debug why e.g. trying to remove a field's border (if defined in the theme) is impossible without wrapping the widget with an additional `Theme` containing explicit `InputBorder.none` values.

### Proposal

As a bare minimum, some form of notice should be added to the `decoration` property informing about the potential need for inserting an applied-property-clearing `Theme` wrapper surrounding the widget.

Ideally though, users should have a less verbose way of specifying whether to merge or fully replace the active `InputDecorationTheme` either when creating, or when applying an `InputDecoration`, e.g. with a `bool? mergeWithTheme` on all constructors, while applying more intuitive defaults (looking at [the `InputDecoration.collapsed()` named constructor](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/InputDecoration/InputDecoration.collapsed.html), that would be a `false` rather than a `true`)

EDIT: Note, I got started creating a small example on https://dartpad.dev to illustrate the issue, but writing code containing a textfield over there is an absolute nightmare once "Run" is executed due to the textfield acting as a cursor magnet making further code edits an incredibly frustrating experience :(

issues.title: Distinguish the key with same scancodes but different logical key.
issues.body:
## Use case
This issue is extracted a common issue from #97582 and #88646.

In some cases, keys with same scancodes (both is zero) will be pressed at the same time with different logical key.
This is caused by emulated shortcut key on Windows.
- On Windows (both win32 apps and web pages on Chromium), pasting from Win+V will emulate Ctrl+V but both scancode is 0.
- On web on Chromium on Windows, the emoji item in the context menu on textarea will emulate Win+period but both scancode is 0.

In #97582 and #88646:
This assertion https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/ab89ce285f76a3fd3a9328ec863f5623cc5ac8bc/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/hardware_keyboard.dart#L444-L447
is fails because PhysicalKeyboardKey uses the pyhsical key to remain pressed keys: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/ab89ce285f76a3fd3a9328ec863f5623cc5ac8bc/packages/flutter/lib/src/services/hardware_keyboard.dart#L392

## Proposal
- The PhysicalKeyboardKey should distinguish these keys.
comments: /cc @dkwingsmt @gspencergoog
Thanks for investigating this! Can I assume that this only happens when the scancode is 0?

- On web
  - On Chromium it seems empty (I forgot to write, sorry), but it is reported as bug (https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=834189).
  - On Firefox, code can be `Unidentified` [(KeyboardEvent: code values | MDN)](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/KeyboardEvent/code/code_values), but in Win+V, Firefox seems to complete code from virtual key.
- On Windows, it seems 0.

I saw these behavior, but I couldn't find any docs to describe this.

issues.title: TextField: Allow showSelectionHandles=true even if readOnly=true
issues.body:
## Use case

I'm using my own custom keyboard to input text into a text field. To prevent the default "soft input panel" (keyboard) to show on focus and other events we can use readOnly=true + showCursor=true. This is according to issue #16863 (see this commenthttps://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/16863#issuecomment-567201637). 

This is working great for me but in text_field.dart we can find this code snippet: `_showSelectionHandles = !widget.readOnly;`. I would like to show the selection handle under the cursor at all times, even if I'm in readOnly mode.

## Proposal

Allow showSelectionHandle to work even if I'm in readOnly mode. I have tested to copy text_field.dart to a separate file in my project, rename it to e.g. TextField2 and then explicitly setting showSelectionHandles=true even if I have readOnly=true and it seems to work great.

My proposal is to allow showSelectionHandle to be set explicitly as a constructor parameter to TextField and that value should be honored even if we are in readOnly mode, e.g. my explicit value should be sent to EditableText widget.

Another alternative is to make _TextFieldState non-private and allow me to override it's behaviour. In this case it would be nice to break up the build method into more methods to more easily override a specific part, e.g. the building of the EditableText widget.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18122438/72615370-d00fcb00-3934-11ea-9447-7447a36afefb.png)
comments: Please don't hesitate to submit a patch to add this; we're unlikely to get to it any time soon since it's a rather esoteric feature for a corner case.

issues.title: Can't scroll left or up with iOS floating cursor (dragging spacebar to move cursor)
issues.body:
Originally reported by @Curvel as part of #89228.

**Repro:**
1. Have a `TextField()`. (Probably an `EditableText` will do.)
1. Use iOS.
1. Type a bunch of text into the input, so that it doesn't fit and has to scroll.
1. Long-press the spacebar, then drag around the keyboard to move the cursor (the "floating cursor" feature; Apple calls it "Turn the onscreen keyboard into a trackpad").
1. Try to move the cursor to the edges of the input, to scroll through the text.

**Expected:**
The text scrolls. This is the native iOS behavior.

**Actual:**
If you move to the end of the visible text — the bottom in a multiline input, or the right/end in a single-line input ­— then it scrolls. But if you move to the beginning — the top in a multiline input, or the left/start in a single-line input — then it doesn't.

### Further context
This is the behavior I see on a recent version from master/main, on iOS 16.

Originally reported in 2021 in #89228, so it isn't new. It's possible details have changed, though: that report only looks at a single-line input, and it says "the TextField doesn't scroll horizontally" without mentioning specifically left/start vs. right/end.
comments: It's because we only send the positions of onscreen characters to the engine. It would be a change to `EditableTextState._updateSelectionRects`. Would need to measure performance impact. Maybe we can send them only if needed (floating cursor is active and near the edge)? But it also opens up a problem where if the field scrolls during the gesture, the selectionrects' positions will no longer be correct.

issues.title: Make TextField grow horizontally first and then vertically
issues.body:
## Use case

I am trying to make a TextField that has the following behaviors:
1. Wraps the text content (both vertically and horizontally).
2. Prioritize growing horizontally until it occupies the maximum width possible.
3. Start growing vertically when there's no room to grow horizontally.

I have an existing implementation that has a behavior that is close to that, but not quite the same:
1. Wraps the text content (both vertically and horizontally).
2. Starts growing vertically when the current width is reached, but when `space` is added, it shrinks vertically and grows back horizontally if it has enough space horizontally.

(This is probably hard to comprehend, will update this bug with screen recordings when I have the time)

The following is my implementation:
```
IntrinsicWidth(
  child: ConstrainedBox(
    constraints: BoxConstraints(
      minWidth: someWidth,
    ),
    child: TextField(
      minLines: 1,
      maxLines: 3,
    ),
  ),
),
```

Seems like there's no way to implement the exact behavior that I want (and my implementation is the closest I can get). I wonder if it's possible to build this behavior into the framework?

## Proposal

To build this behavior into the framework.
comments:
As discussed with @digawp on a different channel, horizontal growth of TextFields is not currently supported.  The plain `Text` widget does support this sort of thing though, so it might be reasonable to add something similar to TextField.

See also [TextWidthBasis](https://master-api.flutter.dev/flutter/painting/TextWidthBasis-class.html).

issues.title: Discussion: Should char messages be redispatched on Win32?
issues.body:
Message redispatching is a mechanism used in add-to-app scenarios that allows messages not handled by Flutter to be handled by outer native applications. On Win32, a key press associated with text input can occur in two forms:

1. `WM_KEYDOWN`, `WM_CHAR`
2. Standalone `WM_CHAR`

For example, when pressing `DeadKey^` followed by an uncombinable character, say `Digit1`, the messages for the second press will be

1. `WM_KEYDOWN(Digit1)`
2. `WM_CHAR('^')`
3. `WM_CHAR('1')`

Where the second message follows the first keydown message, while the third message is a standalone one.

Currently, in the first form, whether the char message will be redispatched follows that of the key down message, which further depends on whether the entire key down event is handled by Flutter framework, while in the second form, the char message is never redispatched.

This leaves inconsistency between the handling mechanism of char messages.

Options to fix this are:
1. **Never redispatch char messages**. Simple. But is it desired to have the outside app only receive keydown/up messages, but not char messages?
2. **Redispatch char messages depending on the result of text handling.** This is harder, because currently text input does not give a result (or it does but the keyboard system never uses them.)

Anyway, the solution should be based on feedback of real add-to-app users, which I'm not aware of many at the time of writing.

(This issue is a note for a TODO.)

issues.title: Make keyboard testing more user-friendly when physical keys are missing
issues.body:
Users often want to test keys that are not directly available on a US keyboard, such as the `Zoom In` key or the `+` key. When the user uses these keys, like

```
await tester.sendKeyDownEvent(LogicalKeyboardKey.zoomIn);
```

The current testing framework throws an exception:

> LogicalKeyboardKey#4cf4c(keyId: "0x10000050d", keyLabel: "Zoom In", debugName: "Zoom In") not found in android physical key map.

This error is frustrating since it does not tell the user how to fix their testing code, despite that their demand feels valid.

 The workaround is to send an arbitrary existing physical key, such as

```
await tester.sendKeyDownEvent(LogicalKeyboardKey.zoomIn, physicalKey: PhysicalKeyboardKey.plus);
```

(Ideally we should allow arbitrary dummy physical key, such as `PhysicalKeyboardKey(0x123456)`, but a real physical key is required to generate the raw key data, which we should really deprecate soon.) The physical key does not matter since none of the code should use this key anyway (it's a key that is not present on a keyboard!)

However, Flutter should be smart enough to solve this for the user.

We should either suggest this workaround in the error message, or probably just forge a dummy physical key for the user in such cases. Or if we have more time, we probably want to design a testing utility class that handle mapping automatically, so that the user does not have to provide physical keys and characters at all.

issues.title: SingleActivator doesn't recognize shortcuts with  some 'alt  + key' combinations
issues.body:
## Use case

A SingleActivator created as:
```
SingleActivator(
   LogicalKeyboardKey.keyG,
   meta: true,
   alt: true,
)
```
doesn't work. Because `alt + G` produces `©`,  and SingleActivator uses `RawKeyEvent` under the hood, the activator expects the logical key for `©`, not `G`. 

A workaround for this, would be to use the `CharacterActivator` as:
```
CharacterActivator(
   '©',
   meta: true,
   alt: true,
)
```

but this doesn't cover the case where you want to use the actual G key.


## Proposal

Create a new type of activator that uses the new `KeyEvent` class, which will do the right thing.
comments:
Thanks for the report @franciscojma86 
In [this](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/116658#issuecomment-1344593376) comment, it is suggested to use `CharacterActivator` since `SingleActivator` is not keyboard layout agnostic. Can you check if the workaround you mentioned could be acceptable and also based on the linked comment ?

It does! But I believe there is still need for an activator that doesn't get fooled by the generated character. If alt + g didn't generate © in a keyboard, the shortcut wouldn't work.  @gspencergoog @dkwingsmt

Yes, @franciscojma86 is right.  For cases involving the option (alt) on macOS, it also means that those shortcuts aren't transportable across platforms: it you wanted Alt+G to work on Windows and Option+G to work on macOS, you currently have to define two different activators: one that is a `CharacterActivator` and one that is a `SingleActivator`.

issues.title: Provide a method for `InlineSpan` to override the styles of its children
issues.body:
## Issue
I'd like to be able to override the styles of all children of an `InlineSpan` tree. Currently each `InlineSpan` has a local representation of its `TextStyle` and `InlineSpan` children, so you cannot simply wrap an `InlineSpan` with another `InlineSpan` and expect the parents `InlineSpan.style` to override it's child's. This is by design, since nested spans can define their own `TextStyle`s.

## Proposal
`InlineSpan` should have some mechanism to override the `TextStyle`s of its `InlineSpan` children. Currently `textScaler` is able to penetrate an entire `InlineSpan` tree because the `InlineSpan.build` method has a `textScaler` member. We can take a similar approach and add individual styling members to the `build` method as needed or we can add a `style` argument that is passed down to all children.

## Use case
For accessibility use cases like updating text spacing settings to match certain standards like WCAG, it is required to be able to change the spacing settings of all text. Currently with `InlineSpan`s this can only be done at the root of the span tree, and if children spans locally define their own styles there is no method to change their styles outside of recreating the span itself. Recreating the span itself is not a robust solution since you can only do this for `InlineSpan`s subclasses you own.

### Use case

### Use case

Native Android Scribe supports several stylus gestures that make editing text with a stylus much easier. Flutter should support them too.

### Proposal

Flutter's Scribe support should include the same [gestures that are supported on Android](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/HandwritingGesture#public-methods):

 * select
 * selectRange
 * delete
 * deleteRange
 * insert
 * insertMode
 * joinOrSplit
 * removeSpace

As well as [previewing](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/inputmethod/PreviewableHandwritingGesture):

 * delete
 * deleteRange

issues.title: Uppercase and case-insensitive LogicalKeyboardKey const values
issues.body:
## Use case

`LogicalKeyboardKey` contains consts only for lowercase letters. The absence of uppercase values not only means that they need to be figured out but also incorrectly suggests case-insensitivity.

## Proposal

I suggest adding `LogicalKeyboardKey` const values for uppercase letters. Also, case-insensitive values as synonyms for both upper and lower case letters could be added to simplify defining case-insensitive shortcuts, etc.
comments: /cc @gspencergoog @dkwingsmt

It is intended design. "Upper" letters and "lower" letters are by its nature the same logical key under different **states**. The state, though, can be acquired through other means. (For now `isKeyPressed` supports whether shift keys are being pressed. Whether CapsLock is on will be added later.)
On the other hand, if you'd like to know the text that a letter key produces, there's a separate "text input" API that text fields connect to.

issues.title: Fragile padding and anchor math for toolbars
issues.body:
For TextSelectionToolbars and SpellCheckSuggestionsToolbar, there is a Padding widget in the Overlay that helps the toolbars naturally position themselves with respect to the device's screen padding.  Usually this is included via an app's Scaffold, but inside of an Overlay it's not there.

Currently, the Padding widget seems to include padding that's related to the toolbar (`_kToolbarContentDistanceBelow`), and the toolbar's anchor also compensates for screen padding that should be done via Padding (`mediaQueryData.padding.top`).

Instead, ideally the Padding would set all of the device screen padding and the anchor would just position itself within it.  That would make the positioning logic much easier and more inline with how Flutter usually handles this.

### References

 * SpellCheckSuggestionsToolbar PR: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/117221
 * TextSelectionToolbar code: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/9102f2fe0bd26db6074ac4a17785296cd341ecb9/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/text_selection_toolbar.dart#L109

issues.title: Flutter on iOS should support semantic autofilling of text fields
issues.body:
See https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/32265 for some context

We currently support password autofill, but not as nicely as we could.  In a native iOS app, autofill works if there's a password box anywhere on the screen.  After a bug fix for the linked issue, we only support it if the box has focus.  We also only support passwords right now, but could be supporting other types of auto filled input (like user names, emails, etc.).

On issue with fixing this is that the framework currently increments the client id for a text input on each attach - and the engine has no knowledge of whether that client ID is for a new or existing text editing state.  The framework also does not pass semantic information for the type of text entry wanted (beyond keyboard types anyway).

Another issue is that the engine reuses the backing fields that the OS can interact with for autofilling for multiple fields. It would need to have a way to track (and free) those backing objects per text field on the framework side.
comments:
> and the engine has no knowledge of whether that client ID is for a new or existing text editing state

I may have misinterpreted this sentence but IIRC each autofillable text field is associated with a stable autofill ID (tied to the Element/State of the field)

issues.title: [VPAT][A11y] disabled password Textfield is missing role in voice over
issues.body: b/429089551 b/428649303 Run the https://github.com/flutter/flutter/tree/master/dev/a11y_assessments and navigate to textfield password label and focus disabled password field VoiceOver announces: "Password, disabled". EXPECTED RESULTS: VoiceOver announces "Password, Text field, disabled" or similar.
comments:
This is happening because disabled text field is [internally classified as read-only](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/fbbc797a6a802f265eca423679ef95c1c02845b7/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/text_field.dart#L1677). And read-only text field has a [non-text-input semantics object](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/d328555151ca0a2e5455c3cc9900b43dc4867d54/engine/src/flutter/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/accessibility_bridge.mm#L267) because selectable text is also internally classified as read-only.

The native iOS text field with VoiceOver announces "Password, dimmed, text field".

This is tricky, the reason that we don't create text input semantics node is because a read-only textfield is used by selectable text. we need a better indication for selectabletext instead of inferring from read-only textfield

issues.title: Add-to-app should support predictive back route transitions
issues.body:
### Use case

As of https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/52302, predictive back works in Android add-to-app everywhere except within Flutter routes (predictive back route transitions was added in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/141373). Add-to-app should support predictive back route transitions too.

### Proposal

Support route transitions in FlutterFragment. This would be similar to how it was added for FlutterActivity in https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/49093, but in FlutterFragment like https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/52302, which added basic predictive back support there.

Predictive back route transitions within Flutter look like this:


https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/8436039/96b6dc20-89c9-45db-b8b4-aec7e996477b

### How to reproduce

Run an add-to-app Flutter app where the Flutter part has multiple routes, like this: https://github.com/justinmc/flutter-add-to-app

Perform a back gesture to go from one Flutter route to another. You should be able to peek at the previous route and cancel the gesture, but instead you just get the typical ZoomPageTransition.

issues.title: `TextField.minLines` documentation is unclear about its `maxLines` interaction
issues.body:
Link to the documentation: https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField/minLines.html

> If this is null (default), text container starts with enough vertical space for one line and grows to accommodate additional lines as they are entered.

This doesn't seem to be true. When `maxLines` is set to a non-null value, `minLine` is effectively set to the same value as `maxLines`. It seems the real meaning of `null` here is to defer to the `maxLines` parameter (which makes the default a bit strange, shouldn't the two parameters be orthogonal?).

This is hinted in the `maxLines` documentation: https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/TextField/maxLines.html

> The input's height is large enough for the given number of lines. If additional lines are entered the input scrolls vertically.
> `const TextField(maxLines: 2)`

but I wasn't able to find anything that directly spells out that interaction when `minLines` is null but `maxLines` is non-null.
comments:
Alternatively, the default can be set to 1 (or 0) to keep true to the documented behavior. 

This would break existing code but I think dart fix is pretty good at doing a fix like this.

issues.title: Do not populate the selection overlay when `EditableText` does not have focus
issues.body:
Currently `EditableText._selectionOverlay` is populated in `_handleSelectionChanged`, which can be called during autofill.

Additionally when a long tap gesture first focuses a `TextField` and requests the toolbar to be shown, but since the primary focus does not change immediately we currently show the toolbar regardless of whether the text field has focus (otherwise long-pressing an unfocused text field won't show the toolbar). But if the text field ended up not getting the focus the toolbar will still show.

Maybe `showToolbar` should also set a private flag so when the text field is finally focused it shows the toolbar.

Corresponds to a `TODO` in `editable_text.dart`.

issues.title: EditableText calls onSelectionChanged even if the selection does not change.
issues.body:
If you add a print statement in this method https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/934dfde42f3f2c8c7665f9874812f7c35d846ff4/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/text_field.dart#L1070

issues.title: Proposal: Support Copy and Paste keycodes
issues.body:
### Use case

It's great to be able to use global keyboard shortcuts for copy and paste that work in GUI apps as well as the terminal. Using Control-C and Control-V often doesn't work because they conflict with bindings within terminals.

A good solution for keyboards they support it is to use the `XF86_Paste` and `XF86_Copy` keycodes. (As they are known on Linux) Keyboards with custom firmware to make these keycodes easy to use are increasingly popular. As long as your apps support it, you can then just always use the "Copy Key" and the "Paste Key". 

Several apps and terminals already support  these keybindings as default options for copy and paste. 

### Proposal

Add support for the `XF86_Paste` and `XF86_Copy` keycodes with text inputs as alternates for copying and pasting.
comments:
A reasonable proposal.

Two things need to be true for this to work:

1) They need to be in our keycode database (which they might already be).
1) They need to be in the list of default key bindings for the appropriate intents. (this is the part I think is missing).

issues.title: MacOS text input plugin should be properly incorporated into the view hierarchy and the responder chain
issues.body:
Today the macOS text input plugin is a subclass of NSTextView which must be in the view hierarchy for it to properly interact with the OS input system.

Moreover the macOS text input plugin calls multiple API that are only supposed to be invoked by the system (`-[NSTextInputContext active]`, `-[NSTextInputContext deactive]`, `-[NSResponder becomeFirstResponder]`, etc.). Setting up the view hierarchy could potentially remove these incorrect API usages.

This issue covers the long-term strategic fix for https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/100272, which was fixed by a short-term workaround.

issues.title: Support text selection programmatically for the SelectableText widget
issues.body:
Flutter currently does not provide a way to select text programmatically for the SelectableText widget. 

On Android we do that using the `Spannable` API, see https://stackoverflow.com/a/41760489/9185192
On iOS we a property to define a selection range, see https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextview/1618615-selectedrange

I [experimented](https://github.com/vashworth/engine/blob/73e7c3f32369ffaca5b9282e0ae8c6d2110c7307/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterViewController_layout_guide.mm) with it briefly, but we should take a closer look. issues.title: Design doc - Decoupling design from text
issues.body:
### Document Link

https://flutter.dev/go/decoupling-design-from-text

### What problem are you solving?

This design doc aims to evaluate the framework's text and text input for areas of improvement. This is especially relevant due to the plan to decouple the `Material` and `Cupertino` libraries from the framework.

This document discusses:
* The future of text selection widgets (`SelectableRegion`) in the core framework after decoupling design libraries from the framework.
* Whether the framework should move/implement text selection UI from design libraries like `Android`/`Material` styled selection handles, `iOS`/`Cupertino` styled selection handles, `Android`/`Material` styled text selection context menu, and `iOS`/`Cupertino` styled text selection context menu in the widgets layer.
* The out of the box experience for `EditableText`.

We are building a custom on-screen keyboard using `TextInputControl` and while typing works great, there seems to be no way to perform a `TextInputAction`, since it only seems to go through the MethodChannel in `TextInput`.

Thus, we currently can't implement a "Submit" button on our custom keyboard.

issues.title: canRequestFocus is a duplicate parameter on TextField

### Use case

Instead of TextField.canRequestFocus, people should use TextField.focusNode.canRequestFocus. The parameter should be removed to avoid the complexity that comes with this duplication.

### Proposal

Deprecate TextField.canRequestFocus. Cases that use it, such as DropdownMenu, should migrate to focusNode.canRequestFocus.

### History

TextField.canRequestFocus was added in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/117504 as an oversight (see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/130011#issuecomment-1624420263).

I attempted to fix this with https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/132273, but I ran into Google test failures that I wasn't sure how to fix.

issues.title: Document why KeyboardListener needs a FocusNode
issues.body: The documentation could be a bit more explicit about why KeyboardListener needs a FocusNode and what is the expected interaction between them.
comments: I wonder if @gspencergoog has a definitive answer here.

Yeah, good point.  In reality, I don't know that it really does need a focus node, since it's just a listener and doesn't affect propagation of the event.  It could just as easily register its key event listener with `HardwareKeyboard.addHandler` or `RawKeyboard.listener`.

The only practical difference is that if it has a focus node, then it can participate in the focus tree so that if a descendant handles the event before it gets there, it won't see that event.

This is a class written 7 years ago, and things have changed a bit since then...
